subject: Ecology
title: Loss of Defensive Behavior
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Loss of Defensive Behavior.

hook.text: Island animals often show no fear of humans. Early sailors called them 'tame' and ate them.

buildup.text: Without predators, fear is costly—it burns energy and limits foraging time.

discovery.text: Island tameness evolves quickly. Even a few hundred years without predators reduces wariness.

twist.text: Galápagos finches still land on tourists' shoulders—they never learned that humans are threats.

climax.text: When predators finally arrive—rats, cats, humans—tame species are defenseless.

punchline.text: Fearlessness is an adaptation until it isn't.

quiz.question: Why are island animals often fearless around humans?
options: They are blind to large animals
